text: The surgeon incises the preperitoneal plane in the patient's left abdomen, revealing the yellow preperitoneal fat underlying the rectus muscle. The incision is made well lateral to the trochar sites, and a second instrument is introduced to assist in guiding the preperitoneal dissection.

The surgeon uses Prograsma in the left hand and scissors in the right to enter the preperitoneal fat, employing blunt dissection with counter traction and gentle pulling. The incisional hernia above is visible and has been de-peritonealized.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to increase the working space for reducing the hernia sac of the umbilical hernia. The dissection crosses the preperitoneal fat in the contralateral rectus, revealing a thinner peritoneum. The robotic articulation aids in contouring along the body wall during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a sequence of slight electrocautery buzz and gentle pushing to separate the peritoneum from its attachments along the anterior abdominal wall. As the dissection progresses laterally, a large flap is opened to allow for mesh placement with adequate overlap, ensuring redundancy in the peritoneum for easy re-peritonealization.

The resident closes the defect using an oblock suture. A 13 by 15 centimeter sheet of flat mesh is placed over the defect and secured to the abdominal wall with cardinal tacks, ensuring it lays flat. The mesh is then re-peritonealized, addressing the visible defect in the peritoneum. The falciform ligament is incised and mobilized to cover the peritoneal defect, with the round ligament ligated using a cautery device to provide additional tissue coverage.
